title: Wood Mackenzie: Era of US$2–4/mmbtu Henry Hub natural gas prices ending as demand and supply conditions shift toward US$5/mmbtu (real) by 2035
gist: Wood Mackenzie forecasts Henry Hub natural gas prices approaching US$5/mmbtu (real) by 2035, up from the US$2-4/mmbtu nominal range of the past decade, as AI-driven power demand adds ~17 bcfd by the mid-2030s while the best US gas acreage depletes and near-zero-cost associated gas fades.

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie forecasts Henry Hub natural gas prices approaching US$5/mmbtu (real) by 2035, up from the nominal US$2-4/mmbtu range that held for most of the past decade, calling the decade of cheap Henry Hub gas over.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: A structural repricing of US gas that lifts input costs for LNG, power and industry and margins for upstream gas producers.
insights.quote: Wood Mackenzie forecasts Henry Hub natural gas prices approaching US$5/mmbtu (real) by 2035
insights.locator: The decade of cheap Henry Hub gas

insights.claim: The shift in Henry Hub price formation is from supply-driven to demand-driven, which changes the risk calculus for any position exposed to US gas economics.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Reframes how to model US gas: demand (power/LNG) now sets the marginal price, so hedge and contract structures must adjust.
insights.quote: The shift in price formation at Henry Hub, from supply-driven to demand-driven, changes the risk calculus for any position exposed to US gas economics.
insights.locator: For upstream operators, LNG project developers

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's rising Henry Hub call has been a defining and contested position over the past 18 months, generating sustained debate among utilities, LNG buyers, upstream operators and financial institutions.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: This is an against-consensus view; positioning ahead of the crowd offers asymmetry if the thesis proves right.
insights.locator: Wood Mackenzie's forecast of rising Henry Hub prices has been a defining

insights.claim: The power sector is now the primary source of new US gas demand, with data centres and AI investment set to add an additional 17 bcfd by the mid-2030s, a near-50% increase from 2025 levels and equivalent to roughly half the gas demand of the entire existing power sector.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: AI load growth is the single biggest demand lever behind the higher-price call; monitor data-centre buildout pace as the key variable.
insights.quote: Power sector demand alone is calling for an additional 17 bcfd by the mid-2030s

insights.claim: US LNG export final investment decisions reached a record high in 2025 with further projects reaching FID in 2026, and US LNG capacity is on track to more than double from current levels, with the US forecast to account for more than one-third of global LNG supply in the early 2030s.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: A doubling of export capacity is durable incremental gas demand that tightens the domestic balance.
insights.locator: Investment decisions for new US LNG export capacity

insights.claim: As the power sector leans more heavily on gas to balance intermittent renewable generation, US gas demand has become structurally more volatile and Henry Hub prices will reflect that volatility.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Higher structural volatility raises option value and widens the trading range, not just the average price.
insights.locator: As the power sector leans more heavily on gas

themes.theme: Supply: maturing plays and fading associated gas

insights.claim: As the highest-quality portions of the Marcellus, Permian and Haynesville are developed, remaining inventory will be less productive and more geologically complex, breakeven costs have stopped falling, and technology gains in mature plays are incremental rather than transformative.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Supply cost inflation from resource maturation underpins the durability of higher prices.
insights.locator: US producers have spent years drilling their best acreage

insights.claim: Associated gas accounted for roughly half of all US gas supply growth over the past decade at near-zero marginal cost, but over the next ten years that share is expected to fall below 20%, leaving supply less responsive to price signals.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: The disappearance of cheap associated gas means dedicated gas drilling (and higher prices) must fill the gap; key supply mechanism.
insights.quote: Over the next ten years, that share is expected to fall below 20%
insights.locator: Associated gas accounted for roughly half

insights.claim: Henry Hub remains a localised benchmark shaped by supply, demand and infrastructure conditions in southern Louisiana, so the forecast does not imply uniform price movements across all US gas markets.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Basis differentials matter; don't extrapolate the Henry Hub call uniformly to every regional US gas hub.
insights.locator: Henry Hub remains a localised benchmark

insights.claim: As the US moves past one-third of global LNG supply in the early 2030s, buyers are already asking questions about over-reliance on a single supply source, and those questions will only get louder.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: US LNG scale flips from commercial advantage to a concentration risk that could spur buyer diversification away from US supply.
insights.quote: buyers are already asking questions about over-reliance on a single supply source. Those questions will only get louder.
insights.locator: The scale of the US LNG position may look like

insights.claim: Key downside risks to the higher-price forecast include non-listed and internationally backed operators accelerating supply, dormant or frontier plays proving viable at scale, and a material slowdown in AI-driven power demand or global LNG trade growth.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: These are the scenarios that would invalidate the bull-gas call; monitor them as the forecast's principal hedges.
insights.locator: The Horizons report examines the principal risks
